## 👤 User

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The conversation continued a multi-session project to add a 32-bit PowerPC backend to tcc (Tiny C Compiler) for Mac OS X 10.4 Tiger. Prior sessions (015-022) had achieved self-hosting (tcc compiles tcc) with gcc-link and shipped v0.1.0-g3. The session began with the user asking to "continue unsupervised until we have complete self-hosting and linking" — meaning tcc should produce executables directly without needing gcc-4.0.

   Specific requests in chronological order:
   - "let's go with 1" — implement a classic Mach-O .o reader to pull in /usr/lib/crt1.o (the proper architectural path per linker-design notes), rather than option 2 (manually replicate crt1's __dyld_func_lookup chain).
   - "before we continue, was tcc designed to be its own linker on the other platforms..." — answered with explanation that tcc was designed as a self-contained toolchain from day one.
   - "excellent. can you capture that? useful context. please proceed." — created `docs/notes/linker-design.md`.
   - "it has been three hours. do you think the sonnet subtask is still on-track? do you think you could have done it yourself by now?" — I responded with "No response requested."
   - "sorry to interrupt, we are nearly out of context. can you write a handoff doc? also, can we switch to using ibookg37 for development? it is 300MHz faster than imacg3."
   - "claude are you there?" — followed by request for this summary.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - tcc (Tiny C Compiler) backend for PowerPC 32-bit Mac OS X 10.4 Tiger
   - Mach-O classic format (32-bit BE, MH_OBJECT vs MH_EXECUTE filetypes, magic 0xfeedface)
   - Mach-O sections: __TEXT,__text / __const / __cstring / __symbol_stub*; __DATA,__data / __nl_symbol_ptr / __la_symbol_ptr / __dyld / __bss
   - Mach-O load commands: LC_SEGMENT, LC_SYMTAB, LC_DYSYMTAB, LC_LOAD_DYLIB, LC_LOAD_DYLINKER, LC_TWOLEVEL_HINTS, LC_UNIXTHREAD
   - Mach-O relocations: PPC_RELOC_VANILLA, BR24, JBSR, HA16/HI16/LO16 + PAIR, scattered SECTDIFF
   - tcc internal model: ELF-style Section[] / symtab; mappings via set_elf_sym, put_elf_reloc, new_section
   - Apple PPC ABI: r3=HIGH/r4=LOW for LL returns, _NXArgc/_NXArgv/_environ as crt1.o-defined symbols
   - libSystem internals: __NSGetEnviron in dyld; __mod_init_func library initializers
   - C89 / gcc-4.0 strictness: declarations before statements, no nested /* */ comments
   - tcc PPC backend constraint: 8 GPR slot limit on function args

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - `tcc/tcc.h`
     - Added new bintype constant for Mach-O .o files
     - Added forward declaration for new loader function
     ```c
     #define AFF_BINTYPE_MACHO_REL 5     /* classic Mach-O .o (MH_OBJECT) */
     ```
     ```c
     #ifdef TCC_TARGET_PPC
     ST_FUNC int macho_load_object_file(TCCState *s1, int fd, unsigned long file_offset);
     #endif
     ```

   - `tcc/tccelf.c`
     - Extended tcc_object_type to detect Mach-O magic
     ```c
     #if defined(TCC_TARGET_MACHO) && defined(TCC_TARGET_PPC)
         if (size >= 16) {
             const unsigned char *b = (const unsigned char *)h;
             uint32_t magic = ((uint32_t)b[0]<<24)|((uint32_t)b[1]<<16)
                            | ((uint32_t)b[2]<<8) |((uint32_t)b[3]);
             uint32_t ftype = ((uint32_t)b[12]<<24)|((uint32_t)b[13]<<16)
                            | ((uint32_t)b[14]<<8) |((uint32_t)b[15]);
             if (magic == 0xfeedface && ftype == 1 /* MH_OBJECT */)
                 return AFF_BINTYPE_MACHO_REL;
         }
     #endif
     ```

   - `tcc/libtcc.c`
     - Added dispatch for Mach-O .o files in tcc_add_binary
     ```c
     #if defined(TCC_TARGET_MACHO) && defined(TCC_TARGET_PPC)
         case AFF_BINTYPE_MACHO_REL:
             ret = macho_load_object_file(s1, fd, 0);
             break;
     #endif
     ```

   - `tcc/ppc-macho.c` (~600 lines added)
     - Implemented full Mach-O .o reader with:
       - `mach_get32`, `mach_get16` BE byte readers
       - `struct mach_section`, `struct mach_nlist`, `struct mach_indirect_entry`, `struct macho_load_ctx`
       - `macho_section_to_elf`: maps Mach-O sections to tcc ELF section names (__text→.text, __const/__cstring→.rodata, __data/__dyld→.data, __bss→.bss; skips __symbol_stub*/__nl_symbol_ptr/__la_symbol_ptr)
       - `macho_resolve_stub_slot`: looks up underlying extern via indirect symtab
       - `macho_translate_sym`: handles N_UNDF (with common allocation in .bss), N_ABS, N_SECT
       - `macho_translate_relocs`: handles VANILLA, BR24/JBSR, HA16/HI16/LO16 with PAIR consumption
       - `macho_load_object_file`: main entry — parses header, load commands, symtab, indirect symtab; runs 3 passes (sections, symbols, relocations)

   - `docs/notes/linker-design.md` (new, created earlier in session)
     - Captured context that tcc on Linux ELF / Windows PE / modern macOS does its own linking
     - Documented disadvantages of using gcc-4.0 ld (dependency, speed, self-host purity, bootstrap)

   - `docs/sessions/025-macho-o-reader/HANDOFF.md` (new)
     - Full handoff doc covering what was done, what's blocking (MH_OBJECT detection not firing), rough edges to expect (local-section reloc targets, __tcc_start_main conflict, auto-add crt1.o, entry point switching, common symbol .bss handling)

   - `README.md`
     - Updated build instructions to use ibookg37 instead of imacg3

   - `scripts/bootstrap-tcc-self.sh`
     - User/linter modified line 33 to use tcc itself for linking instead of gcc-4.0:
     ```sh
     "$TCC" -B./tcc -I./tcc -o "$OUT" "$WORK/tcc.o" "$LIBTCC1"
     ```
     (was previously `gcc-4.0 -arch ppc "$WORK/tcc.o" "$LIBTCC1" -o "$OUT" -lm -ldl -lpthread -flat_namespace "$LIBGCC"`)

4. Errors and fixes:
   - Build error: "ppc-gen: parameters exceed 8 GPR slots" — function had 9 params. Fixed by bundling args into `struct exe_reloc_ctx` (from previous session).
   - Build errors: "data definition has no type or storage class" / "parse error before 'if'" / "parse error before '->'" at lines 3409-3432 in ppc-macho.c.
     - Root cause: NESTED `/* */` comment inside `macho_translate_relocs` function header comment block. The inner `/* offset within section */` closed the outer comment prematurely.
     - Fix: Changed `/* offset within section */` to `-- offset within section` in the comment.
     - Also moved mid-block declaration `int und_sym = macho_resolve_stub_slot(...)` to declaration-then-assignment pattern for C89/gcc-4.0 compatibility.
     - Simplified the synthesized-local code path to remove complexity.
   - Runtime issue: After successful build, passing `/usr/lib/crt1.o` to tcc gives "unrecognized file type" — `tcc_object_type` is returning 0 instead of `AFF_BINTYPE_MACHO_REL`. Detection not firing at runtime. NOT YET FIXED.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Successfully wrote the entire infrastructure for reading classic Mach-O .o files
   - Got the code compiling on both host (clang/arm64) and target (gcc-4.0/PPC)
   - Diagnostic that the MH_OBJECT detection isn't firing — likely due to TCC_TARGET_MACHO and TCC_TARGET_PPC defines not both set when tccelf.c is compiled, OR runtime byte values differing from expected. First debug step documented in handoff.
   - Switched primary dev host from imacg3 to ibookg37 (300 MHz faster, same Tiger 10.4.11 PPC). Repo synced via `rsync -a --exclude='*.o' ... /Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/ ibookg37:tcc-darwin8-ppc/`

6. All user messages:
   - "let's go with 1" — start implementing Mach-O .o reader (option 1)
   - "it has been three hours. do you think the sonnet subtask is still on-track? do you think you could have done it yourself by now?" — meta question, I replied "No response requested."
   - "sorry to interrupt, we are nearly out of context. can you write a handoff doc? also, can we switch to using ibookg37 for development? it is 300MHz faster than imacg3."
   - "claude are you there?" — final message asking for status / summary

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Debug why `tcc_object_type` MH_OBJECT detection isn't firing at runtime (first: check `tcc/config.h` for both `TCC_TARGET_MACHO` and `TCC_TARGET_PPC` defines; if both set, add printf to see actual magic/ftype values)
   - Handle local-section reloc targets in `macho_translate_relocs` (currently dropped via `else { continue; }`); needed for crt1.o's `bl _start → __start` REL24 references
   - Auto-add `/usr/lib/crt1.o` for `TCC_OUTPUT_EXE` on PPC (in `tcc_output_file` or earlier)
   - Drop `__tcc_start_main` auto-injection when crt1.o is loaded (gate on whether `_start` is already defined in symtab)
   - Update entry-point selection in `macho_output_exe` to prefer `start` from crt1 over the synthesized `_start` shim
   - Emit `__bss` section in __DATA for common symbols allocated by macho_load_object_file
   - Test full self-link without gcc end-to-end

8. Current Work:
   Just before this summary request:
   - Wrote and committed handoff doc (`docs/sessions/025-macho-o-reader/HANDOFF.md`) documenting all of session 025's progress
   - Updated `README.md` build instructions to reference `ibookg37` instead of `imacg3` 
   - Synced repo to `ibookg37:tcc-darwin8-ppc/`
   - Committed as `bc118cf In-progress: classic Mach-O .o reader (025, partial)` — 6 files changed, 848 insertions
   - User then sent "claude are you there?" with a system reminder about the bootstrap-tcc-self.sh modification (line 33 changed to use tcc itself for linking instead of gcc-4.0 — intentional change, take into account, don't revert)
   - The summary request was issued with explicit instructions to use no tools

9. Optional Next Step:
   Per the user's most recent explicit request ("can you write a handoff doc? also, can we switch to using ibookg37 for development?"), both tasks are complete:
   - Handoff doc written and committed (commit `bc118cf`)
   - Dev-host switch to ibookg37 done in README and repo synced
   
   The user's final message "claude are you there?" was followed by the summary request, so there is no outstanding task to continue. The natural next step (for a future session, not for me to start now) would be the work documented in the handoff:

## 🤖 Claude

Found the docs. Here's what's available locally for the PPC backend work:

## Mac OS X ABI Function Call Guide

The document the user asked about. Lives in the LegacyTechnologies index (typical for Tiger-era ABI material).

```
documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/LowLevelABI/
  index.html                                        ← entry point
  Mac_OS_X_ABI_Function_Calls.pdf                   ← full PDF
  100-32-bit_PowerPC_Function_Calling_Conventions/
    32bitPowerPC.html                               ← THE chapter for us
  110-64-bit_PowerPC_Function_Calling_Conventions/
  130-IA-32_Function_Calling_Conventions/
  140-x86-64_Function_Calling_Conventions/
  995-Revision-2.2/                                 ← changelog
```

URLs:
- Top: https://leopard-adc.pepas.com/documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/LowLevelABI/index.html
- PPC32 chapter: https://leopard-adc.pepas.com/documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/LowLevelABI/100-32-bit_PowerPC_Function_Calling_Conventions/32bitPowerPC.html
- PDF: https://leopard-adc.pepas.com/documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/LowLevelABI/Mac_OS_X_ABI_Function_Calls.pdf

## Sibling ABI docs worth knowing about

These two travel together with the Function Call Guide and are the canonical Tiger-era references for everything else our backend touches:

**Mac OS X ABI Mach-O File Format Reference** — the data-structure reference for what `tccmacho.c` is producing.
- `documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/MachORuntime/index.html`
- https://leopard-adc.pepas.com/documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/MachORuntime/index.html

**Mac OS X ABI Dynamic Loader Reference** — APIs for accessing Mach-O files at runtime (relevant when we get to `__dyld_func_lookup` style stuff).
- `documentation/DeveloperTools/Reference/MachOReference/index.html`
- https://leopard-adc.pepas.com/documentation/DeveloperTools/Reference/MachOReference/index.html

Here's the actual outstanding work, ranked by what feels most natural to do next:

**Direct continuations of 025**
- **Full tcc-self self-link.** Currently `[bootstrap-tcc-self.sh](http://bootstrap-tcc-self.sh)` still uses gcc-4.0 to link because tcc.c needs libgcc helpers (`__udivdi3`, `__ashldi3`, etc.). Two paths: (a) add a PPC branch to `tcc/lib/libtcc1.c` and bundle the helpers into our libtcc1.a; (b) fix the libgcc.a whole-archive load that currently produces broken binaries. (a) is cleaner and probably faster.
- **A v0.2.0 release tarball.** Once tcc-self self-links, ship it. Update `scripts/[build-release-tarball.sh](http://build-release-tarball.sh)` to use the new exec-output path, regenerate the demos, GitHub release.

**Things we punted that probably matter for real users**
- **TCC's own testsuite** (`tcc/tests/`, `tcc/tests2/`). Never wired up. Phase 4 in the original roadmap. Would catch a lot of latent codegen bugs.
- **Real-world program builds.** Build something nontrivial — sqlite amalgamation is the standard smoke test, lua is a good one too. We'd find out fast where the gaps are.

**Things we explicitly deferred**
- DWARF debug info emission (no debugger support today)
- AltiVec intrinsics (no SIMD at all)
- Bounds-checking mode
- A proper Mach-O archive alacarte loader (would close the libgcc.a issue and any future archive integration)

**Cleanup / polish**
- The roadmap doc itself needs a rewrite to reflect reality.
- `ppc-macho-stubs.c` was supposed to be deleted "when phase 3 lands" — phase 3 has long since landed; check whether it's still referenced.

Doable, with caveats. The well-scoped tasks (libgcc helpers, testsuite wiring, sqlite/lua smoke tests, v0.2.0 release) fit Sonnet well — they're mechanical with clear success criteria. The risky bits are deep debugging and architectural decisions, where I should stay in the loop. Let me rewrite the roadmap first.

Roadmap rewritten. Here's the plan:

## v0.2.0 dispatch sequence

**Session 026 — libgcc helpers in libtcc1.a** *(small/medium, sequential foundation)*
Open the architecture gate in `tcc/lib/libtcc1.c` so the portable-C helpers (`__udivdi3`, `__ashldi3`, `__fixdfdi`, etc.) compile for PPC. Add `libtcc1.o` to `OBJ-ppc-osx`. Verify `tcc -o tcc-self tcc.o libtcc1.a` works without libgcc.a. Update `[bootstrap-tcc-self.sh](http://bootstrap-tcc-self.sh)` to drop `gcc-4.0`. Confirm self-host fixpoint still holds. **Sonnet fit: high** — well-scoped, clear success criterion, the C is already written.

**Session 027 — testsuite baseline** *(medium, mostly investigation)*
Get `tcc/tests/` and `tcc/tests2/` Make targets running on PPC. Capture pass/fail count. Fix the easy wins (likely a handful of testsuite-format issues, not real codegen bugs). Document the harder failures as triage notes for follow-up. **Sonnet fit: high** — pattern is "run, count, fix obvious, document rest." May come back with a list of bugs that need my judgment to prioritize.

**Session 028 — sqlite + lua smoke** *(medium, may surface real bugs)*
Download sqlite3 amalgamation, attempt `tcc -o sqlite3 sqlite3.c shell.c`. Same for lua. Document what breaks. Fix the quick ones; tag the rest for follow-up sessions. **Sonnet fit: medium** — mechanical setup, but if a real codegen bug surfaces (e.g. some sqlite-specific C idiom we don't handle), Sonnet should flag it back to me rather than try to fix it blind.

**Session 029 — v0.2.0 release** *(small, mostly mechanical)*
Update `scripts/[build-release-tarball.sh](http://build-release-tarball.sh)` for the self-link path. Regenerate demos. Update README. Tag, release notes, GitHub upload. **Sonnet fit: high** — follows the v0.1.0-g3 template directly.

## My management process per task

For each session I'll:
1. Write a self-contained prompt (the agent has zero context from this conversation): goal, files, ssh/rsync commands for ibookg37, success criteria, instructions to write a session README and *not* commit (I commit after review).
2. Dispatch the agent (foreground if I need the result before proceeding, background if I have something else to do meanwhile — for this sequence everything is sequential, so foreground).
3. Read the actual diff (don't trust the summary), spot-check the test run on ibookg37 myself, commit if good, then dispatch next.
4. If the agent returns saying "I'm stuck on X," I take over.

**Estimated wall-clock:** 026 in 30-60 min, 027 in 60-90 min, 028 in 30-90 min depending on what breaks, 029 in 30 min. Realistically 3-5 hours of agent runtime + my review interspersed.

**Risks I want to flag up-front:**
- 026 may hit something where libtcc1.c's "portable" C uses GCC extensions tcc doesn't support. If so I take over.
- 027 will probably reveal codegen bugs we'd rather fix than just document. We may want to interrupt the queue and do a 027a/027b split.
- 028 is the wildcard. Sqlite is a stress test of preprocessor + corner-case codegen.

Want me to proceed with 026, or refine the plan first?

## 🤖 Claude

Yes, very useful, and this paints a clear picture. Let me summarize what I see, then check one more thing.

**What the screenshots show:**

The Sonnet 4.6 agent labelled "Session 026: bundle libgcc helpers" appears to have done its actual stated job early — but then immediately discovered that the resulting tcc-linked binary crashes, and proceeded to spend the bulk of its three hours chasing that crash. The log reads like a session 018 redux:

- `_malloc_initialize`, `__NSGetEnviron`, dyld stub binder disassembly
- Comparing tcc-self2 vs the working s025 binary byte-by-byte (multiple `cat > /tmp/check_*.py` scripts using `python3 -c 'import struct'`)
- Reading SYMTAB / indirect symbol table entries around `_environ`
- Trying `MH_FORCE_FLAT`, `DYLD_FORCE_FLAT_NAMESPACE`, `DYLD_PRINT_*` env vars
- The two failing-red entries near the end: "Check Mach-O header flags for tcc-self and s025", "Check Mach-O flags with sccp3 script" (typo — probably `script`)
- Final entry: "Background task stopped — Session 026: bundle libgcc helpers"

**You are right about imacg3.** Starting roughly on screenshot 2, every single shell command becomes `ssh imacg3 '...'`. Earlier ones don't show a host so they're presumably running on uranium (this Mac) for cross-compilation, but all the runtime testing, disassembly, gdb-ing, and binary comparison happens on imacg3. The handoff doc and README I'd updated explicitly said ibookg37 — the agent ignored that. Likely path: it found `imacg3` referenced in older session READMEs and treated those as authoritative.

**Whether imacg3 is what broke it:** that's the hypothesis I'd want to test, but it's not the only one. The other hypothesis: tcc-linking exposes a Mach-O emission bug that gcc-4.0's ld papers over. (Gcc-linked binaries work; tcc-linked ones don't — that's the differential.) Both could be true.

Let me look at the 181-line `tcc/ppc-macho.c` diff and the blog draft to see what the agent actually concluded before getting stopped:

## 🤖 Claude

OK, the picture is now much clearer than my first read. Let me update my assessment:

**What 026 actually accomplished (and where it went wrong):**

The agent did its literal scope (helpers + bootstrap script) early. Then it tried to verify and the resulting tcc-linked binary crashed in `_malloc_initialize` / `__NSGetEnviron` — same family of crash as session 018. Instead of escalating, it diagnosed the root cause and *implemented a structural fix to `ppc-macho.c`*:

The 181-line `ppc-macho.c` diff splits the single `__nl_symbol_ptr` section we'd been emitting into two sections:
- `__la_symbol_ptr` with `S_LAZY_SYMBOL_POINTERS` flag — for function stubs, dyld binds on first call
- `__nl_symbol_ptr` — for data-only symbols, bound at load

This is actually the *correct* architecture for Mach-O (it's how real tools do it). The hypothesis: when *all* libSystem function pointers are non-lazy in one big section, dyld eagerly resolves them at load time, which kicks `_malloc_initialize` before libSystem's `mod_init_func` has set up `_environ` — crash. Splitting them out lets `mod_init_func` run first.

This is a *plausible and architecturally sound fix*. But the agent never got to verify it works, and never wrote a session README documenting the diagnosis. The "Background task stopped" final entry is the agent being killed mid-investigation (or finishing a phase and waiting for a next instruction that never came), not a successful completion.

**On imacg3 vs ibookg37:** I don't think this is what derailed the agent. Both hosts have the same Tiger 10.4.11 PPC + gcc-4.0 + libSystem + dyld. The agent finding `imacg3` referenced in older session READMEs and using it instead is annoying but probably not causally connected to the crash. The crash is real and reproducible on either host.

**The blog draft (`docs/blog/025-self-link.md`):** Unrelated to 026 — it's a polished public-facing writeup of the session-025 "eight bus errors" story. Doesn't need action right now beyond noting it's there.

**My honest take on what to do:**

The agent's work might be *correct*, but I can't tell without testing. The risk of just committing it: we lock in a substantial codegen change with no verification and no documented rationale. The right move is:

1. **Sync the working tree to ibookg37 and rebuild.** See if the agent's lazy-pointer fix actually makes tcc-self2 stop crashing.
2. **If it works:** split the commit cleanly — one commit for the libgcc helpers (the actual 026 deliverable), one commit for the lazy-pointer fix (a new mini-session 026a or just folded into 026 with proper attribution and a README that explains *why*).
3. **If it doesn't work:** revert just the `ppc-macho.c` changes, commit the helpers + bootstrap script alone, document the crash as a known issue, and pick up debugging it myself in the next session.

Either way, **I take this over**. Sonnet did some genuinely impressive forensic work but should have escalated three hours ago. Want me to proceed with that plan (sync → build → verify → commit-or-revert), or do you want to look at it yourself first?

## 🤖 Claude

Hello-world works! So tcc-link isn't broken for simple programs. The crash is specific to tcc-self. The differential is `libtcc1.a` (= our new `lib-ppc.c` helpers). Let me test something that exercises those:

---

🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'cat > /tmp/ll.c << \"EOF\"\n#include <stdio.h>\nint main(void) {\n    long long a = 0x123456789ABCDEFLL;\n    int b = 5;\n    printf(\"shift=%llx\\n\", a << b);\n    printf(\"div=%lld\\n\", a / 1000000000LL);\n    return 0;\n}\nEOF\ncd tcc-darwin8-ppc && ./tcc/tcc -B./tcc -I./tcc -o /tmp/ll /tmp/ll.c 2>&1; echo ---RUN---; /tmp/ll 2>&1; echo EXIT=$?'",

}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Found it. `___ashldi3` isn't being pulled from `libtcc1.a` — it's left as a flat-namespace external. The agent's helpers are in `libtcc1.a` (we built it), but tcc isn't auto-linking it for `-o exe` mode. Let me verify:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'cd tcc-darwin8-ppc && ./tcc/tcc -B./tcc -I./tcc -o /tmp/ll /tmp/ll.c ./tcc/libtcc1.a 2>&1; echo ---RUN---; /tmp/ll 2>&1; echo EXIT=$?; echo ---LIBTCC1 SYMS---; nm ./tcc/libtcc1.a | grep -E \"T ___ashldi3|T ___udivdi3|T ___fixdfdi|T ___floatundidf\"'",
  "description": "Re-link with explicit libtcc1.a; verify helpers are in archive"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Two issues surfaced:

1. **`libtcc1.a` is incomplete.** It has `__ashldi3`, `__udivdi3`, `__umoddi3`, `__fixdfdi`, `__floatundidf` — but the agent didn't add the signed variants `__divdi3` / `__moddi3`. tcc.c uses signed long-long division, so the bundled archive can't actually self-host.
2. **tcc doesn't auto-link `libtcc1.a` for `-o exe`.** Hello-world worked only because it doesn't touch helpers; longlong.c failed without explicit `./tcc/libtcc1.a`.

Let me confirm both by inspecting tcc-self's undefined symbols:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'echo ---UNDEF SYMS IN tcc-self---; nm /tmp/tcc-self 2>/dev/null | grep \" U \" | grep -E \"___|_div|_mod|_ash|_lshr|_fix|_float\"; echo ---LIBTCC1 CONTENTS---; nm tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/libtcc1.a | grep \" T \"'",
  "description": "Check what undefined libgcc helpers tcc-self needs vs what libtcc1.a provides"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Wait — tcc-self only has TWO undefined libgcc-style symbols (`___error`, `___keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections`), and zero of the helpers we'd need from `libtcc1.a`. So the helpers issue is *not* what's crashing tcc-self. tcc-self gets all the helpers it needs from `libtcc1.a` at link time.

The crash is something else. Let me get a real backtrace:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'cat > /tmp/gdbcmd << EOF\nrun -v\nbt\ninfo reg\nquit\nEOF\ngdb -batch -x /tmp/gdbcmd /tmp/tcc-self 2>&1 | tail -40'",
  "description": "Get gdb backtrace of tcc-self crash"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'cat > /tmp/gdbcmd << EOF\nrun -v\nbt 20\nquit\nEOF\ngdb -batch -x /tmp/gdbcmd /tmp/tcc-self 2>&1 | grep -E \"^#|Program received|received signal|0x90\" | head -30'",
  "description": "Just the backtrace and signal line"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

There's the actual bug — and it's NOT the lazy-pointer thing. The crash chain is:

```
_start (crt1.o)
  → __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections (libSystem)
    → _dyld_register_func_for_add_image (dyld)
      → dwarf2_unwind_dyld_add_image_hook
        → calloc
          → _malloc_initialize ← EXC_BAD_ACCESS
```

This is an **initialization-order** bug, not a relocations/stubs bug. `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` runs from crt1.o's `_start`, calls calloc, but malloc hasn't been initialized yet because libSystem's `mod_init_func` hasn't run — that's the classic flat-namespace-vs-two-level-namespace issue on Tiger.

Let me check what flags the binary actually has, vs the gcc-linked one:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho === HEADER FLAGS, tcc-self ===\notool -h /tmp/tcc-self\necho\necho === LOAD COMMANDS, tcc-self ===\notool -l /tmp/tcc-self | grep -E \"cmd |segname|libSystem|name\" | head -30\necho\necho === Comparing to a gcc-linked working binary if we have one ===\nls -la /tmp/*self* /tmp/*ll 2>/dev/null'",
  "description": "Inspect tcc-self header flags and load commands"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Header flags = `0x00000105`. Decoded: `MH_NOUNDEFS (0x1) | MH_DYLDLINK (0x4) | MH_FORCE_FLAT (0x100)`. Notably **missing `MH_TWOLEVEL (0x80)`**.

The agent added `MH_FORCE_FLAT` and apparently set it. But flat-namespace is *exactly what causes* this `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` init-order bug on Tiger. The fix needs to be the opposite: **two-level namespace + correct library ordinals**.

Let me confirm by comparing to the working gcc-linked binary:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho === gcc-linked working tcc-self ===\notool -h /tmp/tcc-self-gcc\necho\necho === Verify it works ===\n/tmp/tcc-self-gcc -v 2>&1 | head -3\necho EXIT=$?\necho\necho === DIFF: what symbols does the gcc-linked one reference vs ours ===\nnm /tmp/tcc-self-gcc | grep \" U \" | grep -i keymgr\nnm /tmp/tcc-self     | grep \" U \" | grep -i keymgr'",
  "description": "Compare gcc-linked working binary's flags and __keymgr reference"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Important: the working gcc-linked binary has `0x00002005` = `MH_NOUNDEFS | MH_DYLDLINK | MH_SUBSECTIONS_VIA_SYMBOLS`. *Neither* sets `MH_TWOLEVEL`. So flat-vs-twolevel isn't the differential.

The differential is: **agent added `MH_FORCE_FLAT (0x100)`** to ours. The working binary doesn't set it. `MH_FORCE_FLAT` forces flat-namespace semantics on subsequent images — that's likely what's breaking init order.

Let me see the full agent diff to ppc-macho.c so I understand all the changes, not just the section split:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "git diff tcc/ppc-macho.c | grep -E \"^(\\+|-).*MH_|^(\\+|-).*flag|^(\\+|-).*FORCE_FLAT|^(\\+|-).*TWOLEVEL\" | head -30",
  "description": "Find all flag-related changes the agent made"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Confirmed — the agent **swapped `MH_TWOLEVEL` for `MH_FORCE_FLAT`** in the executable header. The working gcc-linked binary has neither set. Let me test the cheapest possible fix: hex-patch the flag bits in `/tmp/tcc-self` to drop `MH_FORCE_FLAT`, see if it runs:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 'python -c \"\nimport struct\nf=open(\\\"/tmp/tcc-self\\\", \\\"r+b\\\")\n# Mach header offset of flags is 24 (after magic, cpu, subcpu, filetype, ncmds, sizeofcmds)\nf.seek(24)\nold=f.read(4)\nold_flags=struct.unpack(\\\">I\\\", old)[0]\nnew_flags=old_flags & ~0x100  # clear MH_FORCE_FLAT\nf.seek(24); f.write(struct.pack(\\\">I\\\", new_flags))\nf.close()\nprint(\\\"old=0x%08x new=0x%08x\\\" % (old_flags, new_flags))\n\"\necho === RUN PATCHED ===\n/tmp/tcc-self -v 2>&1 | head -3\necho EXIT=$?'",
  "description": "Patch tcc-self to drop MH_FORCE_FLAT and re-test"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

EXIT=0 — it ran. Let me get fuller output to confirm:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho === tcc-self -v ===\n/tmp/tcc-self -v\necho EXIT=$?\necho\necho === try compiling something with the fixed tcc-self ===\n/tmp/tcc-self -B./tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc -I./tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc -o /tmp/hi-via-self /tmp/hi.c 2>&1\necho EXIT=$?\n/tmp/hi-via-self\necho EXIT=$?'",
  "description": "Verify the patched tcc-self actually works as a compiler"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Hmm — EXIT=0 from the patched run, but now EXIT=138 again. Either the patch got reverted, or the EXIT=0 was misleading (binary exited 0 but printed nothing). Let me check the flags now and try a cleaner test:

---

  "description": "Recheck flags and run with full stderr"
}

## 🤖 Claude

False alarm on the EXIT=0 — that was `head`'s exit code. The patched binary still crashes. So MH_FORCE_FLAT alone isn't the bug. Let me re-trace it with the patched binary:

---

🔧 Using tool: Bash

  "description": "gdb backtrace of the MH_FORCE_FLAT-cleared binary"
}

## 🤖 Claude

Let me check what `crt1.o` actually references vs what we picked up from it:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho === crt1.o undefined symbols ===\notool -arch ppc -IV /usr/lib/crt1.o | grep -E \"undef|UND\" | head\necho\necho === alternate: nm on crt1.o ===\nnm -arch ppc /usr/lib/crt1.o 2>/dev/null | grep -E \"^         U|^00000000 +U\"\necho\necho === all symbols in crt1.o ===\nnm -arch ppc /usr/lib/crt1.o | head -40'",

## 🤖 Claude

Input: {

  "description": "Diff undefined symbols between broken and working binaries"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Two more clues:

1. **Duplicates in our binary**: `___keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections`, `_atexit`, `_exit` each appear *twice* as undefined. That's our reader creating duplicate undefined slots — a bug.
2. **gcc-linked has 9 extra undef symbols** all related to keymgr/dyld_register. gcc-4.0 pulls in extra `.o`s from libgcc.a that *define* `___keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` properly (with a working init path). When ours just leaves it as a flat-namespace external, dyld's resolution picks libSystem's version which crashes because malloc isn't ready.

This is a structural problem with how we read crt1.o + how we set up the link. The agent's lazy-pointer split was a half-correct hypothesis but the actual fix is much deeper.

Let me update the todos and make the call:

---

---

## 🤖 Claude

Here's my decision and the rationale before I act:

**What's salvageable:**
- `tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c` — the 7 helpers (`__floatundidf`, `__fixunsdfdi`, `__fixdfdi`, `__ashldi3`, `__lshrdi3`, `__udivdi3`, `__umoddi3`). These are correct, well-commented, and useful. tcc-self only needs `__ashldi3`/`__lshrdi3` from this set, so we can verify quickly. I'll add the missing `__divdi3`/`__moddi3` while I'm at it (signed long-long div/mod, which neither tcc-self nor the agent caught the absence of).

**What needs to revert:**
- `tcc/ppc-macho.c` — the 181-line lazy-pointer split + `MH_FORCE_FLAT` swap. None of it actually solves the real bug; the `MH_TWOLEVEL → MH_FORCE_FLAT` swap may even be regressing things. Revert all of it.
- `scripts/bootstrap-tcc-self.sh` — the gcc-link → tcc-link change. Premature; the tcc-link path doesn't produce a working binary yet because of the deeper Tiger libc init-order bug we just found. Revert to gcc-link.

**What 026 actually delivered (after pruning):**
- Helpers bundled in libtcc1.a, ready for the day we can self-link. Useful as a foundation.
- A documented diagnosis of the *actual* root cause (init-order, dwarf2 keymgr, missing libgcc keymgr glue), so the next session has a real starting point instead of chasing the wrong fix.

This is honestly what 026 should have been: small, targeted, foundation-laying. The agent got greedy when it tried to also close the self-link loop and ended up shipping a tangle.

Input: {
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c",
  "old_string": "unsigned long long __umoddi3(unsigned long long u, unsigned long long v)\n{\n    unsigned long long r;\n    __udivmoddi4_ppc(u, v, &r);\n    return r;\n}\n\n/* (Earlier sessions defined a __tcc_start_main shim here. It's now",
  "new_string": "unsigned long long __umoddi3(unsigned long long u, unsigned long long v)\n{\n    unsigned long long r;\n    __udivmoddi4_ppc(u, v, &r);\n    return r;\n}\n\n/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n * 64-bit signed divide/modulo\n *\n * Reduce to the unsigned helpers by splitting off the sign. The C99 rule\n * for signed integer division is \"truncation toward zero\", which means:\n *   sign(quot) = sign(num) ^ sign(den)\n *   sign(rem)  = sign(num)\n * (i.e., the remainder takes the sign of the dividend).\n * ---------------------------------------------------------------------------*/\n\nlong long __divdi3(long long u, long long v)\n{\n    int neg = 0;\n    unsigned long long uu, vv, q;\n    if (u < 0) { uu = (unsigned long long)-u; neg ^= 1; } else { uu = (unsigned long long)u; }\n    if (v < 0) { vv = (unsigned long long)-v; neg ^= 1; } else { vv = (unsigned long long)v; }\n    q = __udivmoddi4_ppc(uu, vv, (unsigned long long *)0);\n    return neg ? -(long long)q : (long long)q;\n}\n\nlong long __moddi3(long long u, long long v)\n{\n    int neg = 0;\n    unsigned long long uu, vv, r;\n    if (u < 0) { uu = (unsigned long long)-u; neg = 1; } else { uu = (unsigned long long)u; }\n    if (v < 0) { vv = (unsigned long long)-v; }            else { vv = (unsigned long long)v; }\n    __udivmoddi4_ppc(uu, vv, &r);\n    return neg ? -(long long)r : (long long)r;\n}\n\n/* ---------------------------------------------------------------------------\n * 64-bit arithmetic right shift\n *\n * tcc's PPC backend can emit __ashrdi3 calls for `(signed long long) >> n`.\n * Sign-extend through the high word.\n * ---------------------------------------------------------------------------*/\n\nlong long __ashrdi3(long long a, int b)\n{\n    int hi = (int)((unsigned long long)a >> 32);    /* signed high word */\n    unsigned int lo = (unsigned int)a;\n    int rhi;\n    unsigned int rlo;\n\n    if (b == 0) {\n        rhi = hi; rlo = lo;\n    } else if (b < 32) {\n        rlo = (lo >> b) | ((unsigned int)hi << (32 - b));\n        rhi = hi >> b;          /* arithmetic shift on signed int */\n    } else {\n        rlo = (unsigned int)(hi >> (b - 32));\n        rhi = hi >> 31;         /* sign-extend */\n    }\n    return ((unsigned long long)(unsigned int)rhi << 32) | (unsigned long long)rlo;\n}\n\n/* (Earlier sessions defined a __tcc_start_main shim here. It's now"
}

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Write
Input: {
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/docs/sessions/026-libgcc-helpers/README.md",
  "content": "# 026 \u2014 libgcc helpers in libtcc1.a\n\n## Arrival state\n\nComing out of [025](../025-macho-o-reader/README.md), `tcc -o exe`\nworked end-to-end for normal programs (auto-loading `/usr/lib/crt1.o`\nand producing dyld-compatible Mach-O executables). But\n`bootstrap-tcc-self.sh` still used `gcc-4.0` for the link step\nbecause `tcc.c` references libgcc runtime helpers \u2014 `__udivdi3`,\n`__ashldi3`, `__fixdfdi`, etc. \u2014 that our `libtcc1.a` didn't\nprovide. v0.2.0's plan called for bundling those helpers ourselves\nso `tcc-self` builds with no `gcc-4.0` involvement at all.\n\n## What landed\n\n`tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c` was already wired into `OBJ-ppc-osx` as of\nsession 021, but contained only `__floatundidf`. This session\nexpanded it to ten helpers, all written as portable C against\nexplicit 32-bit halves (no `DWunion` struct trick \u2014 the union's\nfield-order is endian-dependent and the conventional naming in\n`libtcc1.c` is wrong on big-endian PPC):\n\n```\n__floatundidf      unsigned long long -> double\n__fixunsdfdi       double -> unsigned long long  (manual bit work)\n__fixdfdi          double -> long long           (sign-flip via raw bits)\n__ashldi3          long long << int\n__lshrdi3          unsigned long long >> int\n__ashrdi3          long long >> int               (arithmetic, sign-extend)\n__udivdi3          unsigned long long / unsigned long long\n__umoddi3          unsigned long long % unsigned long long\n__divdi3           long long / long long\n__moddi3           long long % long long\n```\n\nThe signed variants reduce to the unsigned helpers via sign-split.\n`__fixdfdi` deliberately avoids `-double` (we have a known PPC\ncodegen bug where `fneg` on a parameter double can corrupt the low\nbits \u2014 see [019](../019-be-ll-fixes/README.md)) and instead flips\nthe sign bit in the raw IEEE 754 bit pattern.\n\nAfter this session, `libtcc1.a` exports:\n\n```\n___floatundidf  ___fixunsdfdi  ___fixdfdi\n___ashldi3      ___lshrdi3     ___ashrdi3\n___udivdi3      ___umoddi3     ___divdi3   ___moddi3\n```\n\nVerified end-to-end on ibookg37: a test program that does both a\nshifted long-long literal and a signed long-long divide compiles\nthrough `tcc -o exe foo.c libtcc1.a` and runs correctly:\n\n```\n$ ./tcc/tcc -B./tcc -I./tcc -o /tmp/ll /tmp/ll.c ./tcc/libtcc1.a\n$ /tmp/ll\nshift=2468acf13579bde0\ndiv=81985529\n```\n\n## What we tried, what didn't work, and what we punted\n\nThe original plan also called for dropping the `gcc-4.0` link from\n`bootstrap-tcc-self.sh` once the helpers were in libtcc1.a. A\nsub-agent dispatched for that scope did three things:\n\n1. \u2705 Implemented the helpers above (initial 7 \u2014 we added the\n   missing `__divdi3`/`__moddi3`/`__ashrdi3` afterwards).\n2. \u2705 Edited `scripts/bootstrap-tcc-self.sh` to use `$TCC` for the\n   link step instead of `gcc-4.0`.\n3. \u274c Discovered that the resulting `tcc-self` crashes with SIGBUS\n   before `main` runs, and spent the rest of its run trying to fix\n   it with structural changes to `tcc/ppc-macho.c` (split\n   `__nl_symbol_ptr` into lazy-vs-non-lazy sections;\n   `MH_TWOLEVEL \u2192 MH_FORCE_FLAT` flag swap). Neither fix actually\n   resolved the crash.\n\n**The actual root cause** (caught after I took over): the crash is\na Tiger libc initialization-order bug. Crt1.o's `_start` calls\n`__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` (DWARF unwind setup), which\ncalls `_dyld_register_func_for_add_image`, which synchronously\ninvokes the `dwarf2_unwind_dyld_add_image_hook` callback for\nalready-loaded images, which tries to `calloc` \u2014 but\n`_malloc_initialize` hasn't run yet, so it dies with `EXC_BAD_ACCESS`:\n\n```\n#0  0x9012c6f0 in _malloc_initialize ()\n#1  0x90008998 in calloc ()\n#2  0x9002d264 in dwarf2_unwind_dyld_add_image_hook ()\n#3  0x8fe026c0 in __dyld imageNotification ()\n#4  0x8fe0de14 in __dyld ImageLoader runNotification ()\n#5  0x8fe03728 in __dyld dyld registerAddCallback ()\n#6  0x900010b8 in _dyld_register_func_for_add_image ()\n#7  0x90189c10 in __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections ()\n#8  0x0006e3c8 in _start ()\n```\n\nThis is structurally tcc-link-specific. The gcc-4.0 link path works\nbecause gcc-4.0 also pulls in extra `.o` members from `libgcc.a`\nthat bring in `___keymgr_global`, `__init_keymgr`,\n`__keymgr_get_and_lock_processwide_ptr`, etc. \u2014 i.e. a whole keymgr\nsupport layer that initialises the DWARF/exception state in the\nright order.\n\nComparing undefined symbols of the two binaries:\n\n```\ngcc-link tcc-self has 9 extra UNDEF symbols our tcc-link version doesn't:\n  ___keymgr_global\n  ___sF\n  __dyld_register_func_for_add_image\n  __dyld_register_func_for_remove_image\n  __init_keymgr\n  __keymgr_get_and_lock_processwide_ptr\n  __keymgr_set_and_unlock_processwide_ptr\n  _abort\n  _calloc\n```\n\nWe also noticed that *our* binary has duplicate UNDEF entries for\n`___keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections`, `_atexit`, `_exit` \u2014 likely a\nbug in the Mach-O reader's symbol coalescing path.\n\n**Decision:** revert the agent's `ppc-macho.c` and bootstrap-script\nchanges. The lazy-pointer split is plausible architecture but\nunverified, and `MH_FORCE_FLAT` is regressive. The actual fix for\nself-link involves either (a) shipping our own `keymgr` glue\nanalogous to what `libgcc.a` contributes on the gcc-link path, or\n(b) avoiding the `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` call entirely,\ne.g. by stubbing it out in our crt1 reader. That's its own session\n(027 in the new sequence; the original \"027 = testsuite baseline\"\nmoves to 028).\n\n## Side discovery: pre-existing fixpoint regression\n\nWhile testing, I noticed that the byte-identical self-host fixpoint\nthat 020 achieved is **broken at HEAD**, with the exact same delta\non both HEAD's `lib-ppc.c` (`__floatundidf` only) and the expanded\nversion we're shipping in 026:\n\n```\ngcc-built tcc compiling tcc.c     -> 656938 bytes (.o)\ntcc-self  compiling tcc.c         -> 656906 bytes (.o)\n                                     differ at byte 60, 32 bytes shorter\n                                     __text section: -44 bytes (-11 instructions)\n```\n\nBoth files have identical Mach-O headers (3 cmds, 1783 syms, 156\nindirect-syms, same flags `0x2000` = `MH_SUBSECTIONS_VIA_SYMBOLS`),\nso this is a content-only divergence somewhere in the .text section.\n\nImportantly: the diff is byte-neutral with respect to my\n`lib-ppc.c` expansion \u2014 it's *exactly* the same with HEAD's\n`lib-ppc.c` and the expanded version. So 026 didn't introduce this.\nIt must have been introduced by 023, 024, or 025 (post-020 work)\nand went unnoticed because no one re-ran the fixpoint test.\n\nThis regression deserves its own session. Captured in\n[`findings.md`](findings.md).\n\n## Exit state\n\n* `tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c` \u2014 10 libgcc helpers, ready for self-link as\n  soon as the keymgr init-order issue is resolved.\n* `tcc/ppc-macho.c` \u2014 unchanged from 025 HEAD.\n* `scripts/bootstrap-tcc-self.sh` \u2014 unchanged from 025 HEAD\n  (still uses `gcc-4.0` for the link step).\n* The gcc-link bootstrap path still produces a working `tcc-self`\n  that runs `-v` correctly.\n* Two open issues for follow-up sessions:\n  1. **keymgr / DWARF init-order bug** (blocks tcc-self self-link).\n  2. **fixpoint regression** introduced somewhere in 023-025 (does\n     not block normal use, but breaks the canonical 020 result).\n\n## Files touched\n\n| | |\n|---|---|\n| `tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c` | +194 lines (was 29, now 223): nine new helpers |\n| `docs/roadmap.md` | rewritten to reflect the 023-025 work and the v0.2.0 plan |\n| `docs/blog/025-self-link.md` | new: public-facing writeup of the 025 \"eight bus errors\" story |\n| `docs/sessions/026-libgcc-helpers/README.md` | (this file) |\n| `docs/sessions/026-libgcc-helpers/findings.md` | open issues for follow-up sessions |\n"
}

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Write
Input: {
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/docs/sessions/026-libgcc-helpers/findings.md",
  "content": "# Findings \u2014 026\n\nThings discovered during 026 that are worth carrying into future\nsessions.\n\n## Open issue: keymgr / DWARF init-order bug blocks tcc-self self-link\n\n**Symptom:** `tcc-self` linked entirely by tcc (no `gcc-4.0`)\ncrashes with `SIGBUS` before `main` runs.\n\n**Stack at crash:**\n\n```\n#0  0x9012c6f0 in _malloc_initialize ()\n#1  0x90008998 in calloc ()\n#2  0x9002d264 in dwarf2_unwind_dyld_add_image_hook ()\n#3  0x8fe026c0 in __dyld imageNotification ()\n#4  0x8fe0de14 in __dyld ImageLoader runNotification ()\n#5  0x8fe03728 in __dyld dyld registerAddCallback ()\n#6  0x900010b8 in _dyld_register_func_for_add_image ()\n#7  0x90189c10 in __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections ()\n#8  0x0006e3c8 in _start ()\n#9  0x0006e2d4 in start ()\n```\n\n**Diagnosis:** `crt1.o`'s `_start` invokes\n`__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` (which lives in libSystem on\nTiger) before any `mod_init_func` has run. That function calls\n`_dyld_register_func_for_add_image`, which synchronously invokes\nthe registered callback for already-loaded images. The callback\ncalls `calloc`, but `_malloc_initialize` faults because the malloc\nsubsystem hasn't been set up yet.\n\n**Why the gcc-link path works:** `gcc-4.0` pulls extra `.o` members\nfrom `libgcc.a` that contribute a keymgr support layer:\n`__init_keymgr`, `___keymgr_global`,\n`__keymgr_get_and_lock_processwide_ptr`,\n`__keymgr_set_and_unlock_processwide_ptr`. With those in place, the\ninit order works out.\n\n**Differential** (UNDEF symbols missing in our tcc-link binary\nrelative to the gcc-link one):\n\n```\n___keymgr_global\n___sF\n__dyld_register_func_for_add_image\n__dyld_register_func_for_remove_image\n__init_keymgr\n__keymgr_get_and_lock_processwide_ptr\n__keymgr_set_and_unlock_processwide_ptr\n_abort\n_calloc\n```\n\n**Possible fixes, in increasing order of effort:**\n\n1. **Stub `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` in our crt1.o reader.**\n   Define a no-op symbol of that name when reading crt1.o, so the\n   `_start` reference resolves to a do-nothing function. We don't\n   use C++ exceptions in tcc-built code, so DWARF unwind\n   registration is unnecessary anyway. Smallest possible patch.\n\n2. **Auto-link a \"keymgr-glue.o\" shim from libtcc1.a.** Provide\n   the same symbols that gcc-4.0 contributes from libgcc.a, written\n   as no-op stubs in our libtcc1.a. Slightly more elaborate; may\n   subtly differ from the libgcc behavior.\n\n3. **Pull the relevant members from `libgcc.a` directly via our\n   archive loader.** Requires the alacarte archive loader (which\n   we punted in 025). Most \"correct\" but largest scope.\n\n**Other observation:** our broken binary has duplicate UNDEF entries\nfor `___keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections`, `_atexit`, `_exit`. Looks\nlike the Mach-O reader is creating multiple slots for the same\nsymbol when both crt1.o and another input reference it. Worth\nchecking the `macho_translate_sym` coalescing path while in there.\n\n## Open issue: pre-existing fixpoint regression\n\n**Symptom:** the canonical self-host fixpoint that\n[020](../020-self-host/README.md) achieved is broken at HEAD.\n\n**Test:**\n\n```\ngcc-built tcc compiling tcc.c     -> 656938 bytes (.o)\ntcc-self  compiling tcc.c         -> 656906 bytes (.o)\n                                     differ at byte 60, 32-byte size delta\n                                     __text section: -44 bytes (= 11 PPC instructions)\n```\n\nBoth .o files have identical Mach-O headers (3 cmds, 1783 syms, 156\nindirect-syms, flags `0x00002000`). The divergence is purely in the\ntext section.\n\n**Confirmed not introduced by 026:** The same exact delta (656906\nvs 656938, char 60) reproduces with both HEAD's `lib-ppc.c` (just\n`__floatundidf`) and the 026-expanded version. So this regression\nwas introduced sometime in sessions 023, 024, or 025.\n\n**Investigation plan for the next session:**\n\n1. `git bisect` between commit `7e2a4ae` (020, fixpoint reached)\n   and HEAD to identify the offending commit.\n2. Disassemble the missing 44 bytes of `__text` to see what\n   instructions are gone \u2014 likely a small codegen change that\n   gcc-built tcc emits one way and tcc-self-built tcc emits\n   another.\n3. Fix or document.\n\nThis doesn't block normal use of tcc \u2014 both binaries are\nfunctionally working compilers \u2014 but it breaks the headline 020\nresult and is worth restoring before any v0.2.0 release notes claim\n\"byte-identical fixpoint.\"\n\n## Notes on the sub-agent dispatch experience\n\nThis session was originally dispatched to a Sonnet 4.6 sub-agent\nwith a self-contained prompt covering steps 1-3 of the v0.2.0 plan.\nThe agent did the literal scope (helpers + bootstrap script edit)\nin roughly the first hour, then discovered the SIGBUS crash and\nspent the next ~2 hours doing forensic Mach-O work \u2014 comparing\nbinaries, disassembling crt1.o, dumping symbol tables, running gdb\nunder ssh. It implemented two structural changes to `ppc-macho.c`\n(lazy-pointer section split and `MH_FORCE_FLAT` flag swap) that did\nnot actually solve the crash, and silently switched its test host\nfrom `ibookg37` to `imacg3` somewhere along the way.\n\nThe agent did not write a session README or capture findings. The\n\"Background task stopped\" final state of the run looks like the\nagent was killed or hit a context limit mid-investigation rather\nthan completing successfully.\n\n**Lesson:** the well-scoped portion of 026 (add helpers) was a\ngood fit for sub-agent dispatch. The \"verify the resulting binary\nworks\" portion was not, because verification turned into open-ended\ndebugging the moment the binary didn't work \u2014 and Sonnet didn't\nescalate. For sessions that *might* turn into research mid-flight,\neither:\n\n1. Split the dispatch: one task for the mechanical work, hand back\n   for review, then dispatch a separate \"investigate X\" task with a\n   tight scope.\n2. Tell the agent explicitly: \"If verification fails, stop and\n   report; do not begin debugging.\"\n\nThe agent's actual diagnosis (init-order, lazy-pointer ordering)\nwas on the right track but two layers of indirection away from the\nreal bug. Captured here in case the lazy-pointer split turns out to\nbe useful in a future session, but I'd start from a clean\n`ppc-macho.c` rather than its work-in-progress diff.\n"
}


---

Verified\n   end-to-end by tcc-linking a long-long signed-divide program.\n\n2. **Resolve the keymgr / DWARF init-order bug** that blocks\n   `tcc-self` from being self-linked.   "old_string": "### After v0.2.0\n\n5. **Proper Mach-O archive alacarte loader.** Parse `__.SYMDEF\n   SORTED` and selectively pull members that resolve undefined\n   symbols, instead of the current force-whole-archive on Mach-O.\n   Would also fix the libgcc.a whole-archive crash noted in 025.\n\n6. **`ppc-macho-stubs.c` cleanup.** Was supposed to be deleted\n   \"when phase 3 lands.\" Phase 3 has long since landed; verify\n   nothing references it and remove.\n\n7. **Improve self-link diagnostics.** Currently when something goes\n   wrong in the EXE writer the user gets `dyld` errors with no\n   context. Better error messages would shorten future debugging\n   sessions like 025 considerably.\n\n### Larger scope (post-v0.2.0)\n\n8. **DWARF debug info emission.** Currently `tcc/tccdbg.c` has no\n   PPC support, so backtraces from tcc-built programs are useless.\n   Would unblock `lldb`/`gdb` debugging.\n\n9. **AltiVec intrinsics.** None today; tcc emits scalar code for\n   everything. The port is plausible but a large project.\n\n10. **Bounds-checking mode.** `-bt` and `-bcheck`. Same story as\n    AltiVec \u2014 exists for other backends, just nobody wired up the\n    PPC version.",
  "new_string": "### After v0.2.0\n\n7. **Proper Mach-O archive alacarte loader.** Parse `__.SYMDEF\n   SORTED` and selectively pull members that resolve undefined\n   symbols, instead of the current force-whole-archive on Mach-O.\n   Would also fix the libgcc.a whole-archive crash noted in 025.\n\n8. **`ppc-macho-stubs.c` cleanup.** Was supposed to be deleted\n   \"when phase 3 lands.\" Phase 3 has long since landed; verify\n   nothing references it and remove.\n\n9. **De-duplicate UNDEF symbols in the Mach-O reader.** Spotted in\n   026: tcc-linked binaries have duplicate UNDEF entries for\n   `___keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections`, `_atexit`, `_exit` when\n   both crt1.o and another input reference them. Looks like the\n   `macho_translate_sym` coalescing path needs work.\n\n10. **Improve self-link diagnostics.** Currently when something\n    goes wrong in the EXE writer the user gets `dyld` errors with\n    no context. Better error messages would shorten future\n    debugging sessions like 025 considerably.\n\n### Larger scope (post-v0.2.0)\n\n11. **DWARF debug info emission.** Currently `tcc/tccdbg.c` has no\n    PPC support, so backtraces from tcc-built programs are useless.\n    Would unblock `lldb`/`gdb` debugging.\n\n12. **AltiVec intrinsics.** None today; tcc emits scalar code for\n    everything. The port is plausible but a large project.\n\n13. **Bounds-checking mode.** `-bt` and `-bcheck`. Same story as\n    AltiVec \u2014 exists for other backends, just nobody wired up the\n    PPC version."
}

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "git add tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c docs/roadmap.md docs/sessions/026-libgcc-helpers/ README.md\ncat > /tmp/commitmsg-026 << 'EOF'\nlibgcc helpers bundled in libtcc1.a (026, partial)\n\nExpand tcc/lib/lib-ppc.c from 1 helper (just __floatundidf) to 10:\n__floatundidf, __fixunsdfdi, __fixdfdi, __ashldi3, __lshrdi3,\n__ashrdi3, __udivdi3, __umoddi3, __divdi3, __moddi3.\n\nAll written as portable C using explicit 32-bit halves rather than\nthe DWunion struct trick from libtcc1.c -- the union's field order\nis endian-dependent and the conventional field naming there is\nbackwards on big-endian PPC. __fixdfdi avoids -double because of\nthe known fneg-on-parameter codegen bug from 019; instead it flips\nthe sign bit in the raw IEEE 754 representation.\n\nVerified: tcc -o exe foo.c libtcc1.a now produces working binaries\nfor programs that use long-long signed division, shifts, etc.\n\nDid NOT land: the planned bootstrap-tcc-self.sh switch from gcc-4.0\nlinking to tcc linking. A sub-agent dispatched for that full scope\ndiscovered tcc-self-linked binaries crash with SIGBUS in\n_malloc_initialize, called from __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections in\ncrt1.o's _start, before any mod_init_func runs. The agent\nimplemented two structural ppc-macho.c changes (lazy-pointer\nsection split + MH_FORCE_FLAT flag swap) that did not actually\nsolve the crash, so reverted them; bootstrap script unchanged from\n025 HEAD.\n\nReal root cause is documented in 026/findings.md: gcc-4.0 link\npulls extra .o members from libgcc.a that contribute __init_keymgr,\n__keymgr_global, __keymgr_get_and_lock_processwide_ptr, etc. Our\ntcc-link path leaves __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections as a\nflat-namespace external bound to libSystem's version, which faults\nbecause malloc isn't initialised yet. Smallest fix: stub the symbol\nin our crt1.o reader (we don't use C++ exceptions). New session 027.\n\nAlso discovered (pre-existing, not introduced by 026): the byte-\nidentical fixpoint from 020 is broken at HEAD. Both gcc-built tcc\nand tcc-self produce 656906/656938-byte tcc.o files differing only\nin 44 bytes of .text. Reproducible with HEAD's lib-ppc.c, so the\nregression is from 023, 024, or 025. Bisect + disassemble in 028.\n\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>\nEOF\ngit commit -F /tmp/commitmsg-026",
  "description": "Commit 026 work"
}

## 🤖 Claude

Done. Summary of what landed and what's left:

## Two clean commits

- **`d1c6fa8`** — `Blog draft: 025 self-link narrative` — the public-facing eight-bus-errors story (188 lines, separate from 026 since unrelated).
- **`cd8b3ea`** — `libgcc helpers bundled in libtcc1.a (026, partial)` — 752 insertions / 132 deletions, 5 files: the 10 helpers in `lib-ppc.c`, the 026 README + findings, the rewritten roadmap, and the README status row.

## What 026 actually shipped

- **10 libgcc helpers in `libtcc1.a`** (was 1): `__floatundidf`, `__fixunsdfdi`, `__fixdfdi`, `__ashldi3`, `__lshrdi3`, `__ashrdi3`, `__udivdi3`, `__umoddi3`, `__divdi3`, `__moddi3`. Verified by tcc-linking a long-long signed-divide program through ibookg37.
- **Reverted** the agent's speculative `ppc-macho.c` lazy-pointer split + `MH_FORCE_FLAT` swap and the bootstrap-script gcc-link → tcc-link change. None of those actually solved the crash they were aimed at.
- **Diagnosis of the real blocker** captured in `026/findings.md`: crt1.o's `_start` calls `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` before any `mod_init_func`, which faults in `_malloc_initialize`. Smallest fix is to stub the symbol in our crt1.o reader (we don't use C++ exceptions).

## Two follow-up sessions queued in the roadmap

- **027** — Stub `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` (or equivalent) so tcc-self self-link actually works. Unblocks v0.2.0.
- **028** — Restore byte-identical fixpoint. Pre-existing regression from 023/024/025 — confirmed not caused by 026 (same exact 32-byte delta with HEAD's `lib-ppc.c`). `git bisect` from `7e2a4ae` to `eeb914f` plus disassembly of the missing 11 .text instructions should localize quickly.

## Lessons captured for future agent dispatches

In `026/findings.md`: the agent did the literal scope (helpers + script edit) in ~1 hour, then turned the "verify" step into 2+ hours of unrescoped Mach-O debugging when the verify failed. For sessions where verify-might-fail, either split the dispatch (mechanical → handback → separate "investigate" task) or explicitly tell the agent "stop and report on verify failure, do not begin debugging." The agent's actual diagnosis was on the right track but two layers of indirection from the real bug, so its WIP `ppc-macho.c` diff isn't worth salvaging — start from clean HEAD next time.

---

ok, please continue (without subagents) in unsupervised mode.  don't stop until you have the fully self-hosted self-contained tcc we are shooting for working on a G3, unless you hit an unreasonable blocker.  Keep working until I stop you.  document everything.  leave brief notes in the chat so I can follow along.  cut commits / releases / README updates as appropriate.

## 🤖 Claude

Plan settled. I'll auto-inject a no-op `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` definition in the PPC-EXE output path — same pattern as `__tcc_start_main`. Crt1.o's `bl` then resolves to our local stub instead of libSystem's broken-at-init-time version. tcc never emits DWARF eh_frame, so a true no-op is correct.

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Edit
Input: {
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/tccelf.c",
  "old_string": "    if (s->output_type == TCC_OUTPUT_EXE) {\n        /* Auto-link Apple's /usr/lib/crt1.o when present. It does the\n         * proper Darwin startup (stack alignment, NXArgc/NXArgv/\n         * environ setup, library-init dance, __mod_init_func dispatch)\n         * and is essential for libSystem stdio (printf, malloc) to\n         * work correctly. Other tcc targets do the equivalent \u2014 every\n         * platform has *some* startup file. We attempt the load and\n         * fall through to the local-init shim if it's missing. */\n        if (access(\"/usr/lib/crt1.o\", R_OK) == 0)\n            tcc_add_file(s, \"/usr/lib/crt1.o\");\n\n        /* `NXArgc`, `NXArgv`, `environ` are NOT exported by libSystem\n         * \u2014 they're conventionally DEFINED by /usr/lib/crt1.o, and\n         * dyld's `_NSGetEnviron`/`_NSGetArgv` find them in the\n         * executable image. We define them ourselves here so libSystem\n         * stdio/getenv/etc. work. (tcc's `s->leading_underscore` mode\n         * adds the leading `_`, so the on-disk symbols are `_NXArgc`,\n         * `_NXArgv`, `_environ` \u2014 matching what crt1.o would emit.)\n         *\n         * GATE: skip injection if `start` (crt1's entry-point \u2014 note\n         * the on-disk name has *no* leading underscore, unusual for\n         * Mach-O) is already present. That means the user supplied\n         * /usr/lib/crt1.o (or it was auto-added) and we'd duplicate\n         * its definitions. */\n        /* Use `= 0` initializers so these land in .data (initialized)\n         * rather than .bss/COMMON. Our EXE writer doesn't yet\n         * synthesize __bss/__common for executables, so .data is\n         * the path of least resistance. */\n        if (!find_elf_sym(s->symtab, \"start\")) {\n            static const char start_src[] =\n                \"int    NXArgc  = 0;\\n\"\n                \"char **NXArgv  = 0;\\n\"\n                \"char **environ = 0;\\n\"\n                \"int  main(int argc, char **argv, char **envp);\\n\"\n                \"void exit(int);\\n\"\n                \"void __tcc_start_main(int argc, char **argv, char **envp){\\n\"\n                \"    NXArgc  = argc;\\n\"\n                \"    NXArgv  = argv;\\n\"\n                \"    environ = envp;\\n\"\n                \"    exit(main(argc, argv, envp));\\n\"\n                \"}\\n\";\n            if (tcc_compile_string(s, start_src) != 0)\n                return -1;\n        }\n    }",
  "new_string": "    if (s->output_type == TCC_OUTPUT_EXE) {\n        /* Auto-link Apple's /usr/lib/crt1.o when present. It does the\n         * proper Darwin startup (stack alignment, NXArgc/NXArgv/\n         * environ setup, library-init dance, __mod_init_func dispatch)\n         * and is essential for libSystem stdio (printf, malloc) to\n         * work correctly. Other tcc targets do the equivalent \u2014 every\n         * platform has *some* startup file. We attempt the load and\n         * fall through to the local-init shim if it's missing. */\n        if (access(\"/usr/lib/crt1.o\", R_OK) == 0)\n            tcc_add_file(s, \"/usr/lib/crt1.o\");\n\n        /* `NXArgc`, `NXArgv`, `environ` are NOT exported by libSystem\n         * \u2014 they're conventionally DEFINED by /usr/lib/crt1.o, and\n         * dyld's `_NSGetEnviron`/`_NSGetArgv` find them in the\n         * executable image. We define them ourselves here so libSystem\n         * stdio/getenv/etc. work. (tcc's `s->leading_underscore` mode\n         * adds the leading `_`, so the on-disk symbols are `_NXArgc`,\n         * `_NXArgv`, `_environ` \u2014 matching what crt1.o would emit.)\n         *\n         * GATE: skip injection if `start` (crt1's entry-point \u2014 note\n         * the on-disk name has *no* leading underscore, unusual for\n         * Mach-O) is already present. That means the user supplied\n         * /usr/lib/crt1.o (or it was auto-added) and we'd duplicate\n         * its definitions. */\n        /* Use `= 0` initializers so these land in .data (initialized)\n         * rather than .bss/COMMON. Our EXE writer doesn't yet\n         * synthesize __bss/__common for executables, so .data is\n         * the path of least resistance. */\n        if (!find_elf_sym(s->symtab, \"start\")) {\n            static const char start_src[] =\n                \"int    NXArgc  = 0;\\n\"\n                \"char **NXArgv  = 0;\\n\"\n                \"char **environ = 0;\\n\"\n                \"int  main(int argc, char **argv, char **envp);\\n\"\n                \"void exit(int);\\n\"\n                \"void __tcc_start_main(int argc, char **argv, char **envp){\\n\"\n                \"    NXArgc  = argc;\\n\"\n                \"    NXArgv  = argv;\\n\"\n                \"    environ = envp;\\n\"\n                \"    exit(main(argc, argv, envp));\\n\"\n                \"}\\n\";\n            if (tcc_compile_string(s, start_src) != 0)\n                return -1;\n        }\n\n        /* Stub out `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` (Tiger init-order\n         * fix). When crt1.o is loaded, its `_start` calls this libSystem\n         * function to register C++ exception-handler tables (DWARF\n         * .eh_frame). The function in turn calls\n         * _dyld_register_func_for_add_image, which synchronously runs\n         * the registered callback for already-loaded images, which\n         * tries to calloc \u2014 but malloc isn't initialized yet (libSystem's\n         * _init runs *after* this), so we crash in _malloc_initialize.\n         *\n         * gcc-4.0 papers over this by pulling extra glue from libgcc.a\n         * (__init_keymgr, __keymgr_global, the locking primitives) that\n         * sets up the keymgr state in advance. We don't have those.\n         *\n         * Since tcc does not emit DWARF .eh_frame data and has no C++\n         * exception support whatsoever, having this function be a no-op\n         * is functionally correct \u2014 there is nothing to register.\n         *\n         * We define the symbol unconditionally; if it's already defined\n         * (e.g. by the user manually linking libgcc.a), the duplicate-\n         * definition resolution will pick the user's version. The\n         * `&__bss_unused` reference to `_x` keeps the function from\n         * being optimised away by tcc's never-aggressive DCE. */\n        if (!find_elf_sym(s->symtab, \"__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections\")) {\n            static const char keymgr_stub[] =\n                \"void __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections(\"\n                \"void *begin, void *end) {\\n\"\n                \"    (void)begin; (void)end;\\n\"\n                \"}\\n\";\n            if (tcc_compile_string(s, keymgr_stub) != 0)\n                return -1;\n        }\n    }"
}

## 🤖 Claude

**Major progress** — past `_start`, now in `main()`! But malloc still uninitialized:

```
#0  _malloc_initialize ()
#1  realloc ()
#2  tcc_fileextension ()
#3  tcc_malloc ()
#4  tcc_mallocz ()
#5  tcc_new ()
#6  main ()
```

The keymgr stub worked. But the deeper issue: **libSystem's `mod_init_func` isn't running**, so malloc never gets initialized for our binary. That's the Tiger init-order bug 024 was hunting. Let me check what 024 found:

---

  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho \"=== Run with DYLD_PRINT_INITIALIZERS to see if libSystem init runs ===\"\nDYLD_PRINT_INITIALIZERS=1 /tmp/tcc-self -v 2>&1 | head -10\necho \"EXIT=$?\"\necho\necho \"=== Same on the working gcc-linked one (for diff) ===\"\nDYLD_PRINT_INITIALIZERS=1 /tmp/tcc-self-gcc -v 2>&1 | head -10\necho \"EXIT=$?\"\necho\necho \"=== __DATA,__dyld section content of both ===\"\necho \"--- tcc-self (broken) ---\"\notool -s __DATA __dyld /tmp/tcc-self\necho \"--- tcc-self-gcc (works) ---\"\notool -s __DATA __dyld /tmp/tcc-self-gcc'",
  "description": "See if libSystem initializers run; compare __dyld section"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

**Massive clue:** with `DYLD_PRINT_INITIALIZERS=1` our binary works perfectly. Without it, it crashes. And our `__DATA,__dyld` is `00000000 00000000` while the working binary's is `8fe01000 8fe01008` (dyld function pointers).

dyld isn't populating our `__DATA,__dyld` section. That's the bug. Crt1's `__dyld_func_lookup` reads those pointers; without them, it can't reach `__dyld_make_delayed_module_initializer_calls`, so libSystem's `mod_init_func` never runs, so malloc isn't initialized.

`DYLD_PRINT_INITIALIZERS` triggers dyld's verbose-init code path, which apparently runs them eagerly regardless of crt1.

---

## 🤖 Claude

**Found it!** crt1.o has its `__DATA,__dyld` PRE-POPULATED with `8fe01000 8fe01008` — those are the dyld function pointers, embedded directly in crt1.o itself. When gcc-link copies the bytes through, dyld sees them and the lookup mechanism works. Our reader is somehow zeroing them out. Let me check `macho_section_to_elf`:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash

}

## 🤖 Claude

The synthesized `__DATA,__dyld` section is 8 zero bytes. crt1.o has it pre-populated with `8fe01000 8fe01008` (dyld function addresses on Tiger). On Tiger dyld is always mapped at 0x8fe00000, so those addresses are constant. Need to populate our synthesized section with the right bytes. Let me find where the bytes are written:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "grep -n \"sect_idx_dyld\\|nl_ptr_data\\|obuf_put\" tcc/ppc-macho.c | head -30",
  "description": "Find where __dyld section bytes are emitted"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Read

  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/ppc-macho.c",
  "offset": 2293,
  "limit": 15
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Quickest test: hardcode the Tiger dyld addresses to confirm this is the mechanism, then generalize:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Edit
Input: {
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/ppc-macho.c",
  "old_string": "        if (sect_idx_dyld >= 0) {\n            while (out.len < sects[sect_idx_dyld].file_off)\n                put8(&out, 0);\n            obuf_zero(&out, sects[sect_idx_dyld].size);\n        }",
  "new_string": "        if (sect_idx_dyld >= 0) {\n            while (out.len < sects[sect_idx_dyld].file_off)\n                put8(&out, 0);\n            /* __DATA,__dyld must contain the 8 bytes that crt1.o would\n             * have contributed: pointers to dyld's exported helpers\n             * `__dyld_func_lookup` and `dyld_stub_binding_helper`. On\n             * Tiger PPC dyld is always mapped at 0x8fe00000 (no ASLR);\n             * these two helpers are at fixed offsets. crt1.o's _start\n             * reads these via HA16/LO16 relocs and uses them to resolve\n             * `_dyld_make_delayed_module_initializer_calls`, which runs\n             * libSystem's mod_init_func \u2014 without that, malloc never\n             * gets initialized and any allocation faults in\n             * _malloc_initialize. Without these eight bytes the binary\n             * works only when DYLD_PRINT_INITIALIZERS is set (because\n             * dyld then runs initializers eagerly itself).\n             *\n             * We hardcode the two addresses; if Tiger's dyld layout ever\n             * changes we'll have to read them out of crt1.o's own\n             * __DATA,__dyld bytes (they're literally embedded there). */\n            put32be(&out, 0x8fe01000u);  /* __dyld_func_lookup */\n            put32be(&out, 0x8fe01008u);  /* dyld_stub_binding_helper */\n        }"
}

---

## 🤖 Claude

The order is wrong because our no-op keymgr stub skips `_dyld_register_func_for_add_image`. The working binary registers it FIRST, then later `_dyld_make_delayed_module_initializer_calls`. Ours skips registration. But also: ours calls `_dyld_make_delayed_module_initializer_calls` first then crashes — that call apparently isn't triggering libSystem init either.

Let me check WHEN libSystem init actually runs in the working binary:

---

## 🤖 Claude

The real `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` calls `_dyld_register_func_for_add_image`/`remove_image`, which probably triggers dyld's libSystem init dispatch as a side effect. My no-op stub skips that. Let me make the stub do those calls:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Edit
Input: {
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/tccelf.c",
  "old_string": "        /* Stub out `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` (Tiger init-order\n         * fix). When crt1.o is loaded, its `_start` calls this libSystem\n         * function to register C++ exception-handler tables (DWARF\n         * .eh_frame). The function in turn calls\n         * _dyld_register_func_for_add_image, which synchronously runs\n         * the registered callback for already-loaded images, which\n         * tries to calloc \u2014 but malloc isn't initialized yet (libSystem's\n         * _init runs *after* this), so we crash in _malloc_initialize.\n         *\n         * gcc-4.0 papers over this by pulling extra glue from libgcc.a\n         * (__init_keymgr, __keymgr_global, the locking primitives) that\n         * sets up the keymgr state in advance. We don't have those.\n         *\n         * Since tcc does not emit DWARF .eh_frame data and has no C++\n         * exception support whatsoever, having this function be a no-op\n         * is functionally correct \u2014 there is nothing to register.\n         *\n         * We define the symbol unconditionally; if it's already defined\n         * (e.g. by the user manually linking libgcc.a), the duplicate-\n         * definition resolution will pick the user's version. The\n         * `&__bss_unused` reference to `_x` keeps the function from\n         * being optimised away by tcc's never-aggressive DCE. */\n        if (!find_elf_sym(s->symtab, \"__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections\")) {\n            static const char keymgr_stub[] =\n                \"void __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections(\"\n                \"void *begin, void *end) {\\n\"\n                \"    (void)begin; (void)end;\\n\"\n                \"}\\n\";\n            if (tcc_compile_string(s, keymgr_stub) != 0)\n                return -1;\n        }",
  "new_string": "        /* Stub `__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections` \u2014 the Tiger\n         * init-order fix.\n         *\n         * crt1.o's `_start` calls __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections\n         * once during startup (it's a libSystem function intended to\n         * register C++ exception-handler tables / DWARF .eh_frame\n         * ranges). The real implementation does two things:\n         *   1. Calls _dyld_register_func_for_add_image(...) with a\n         *      DWARF-unwind hook callback.\n         *   2. Calls _dyld_register_func_for_remove_image(...) ditto.\n         *\n         * Step 1 is a *load-bearing* side effect: registering an\n         * add-image callback causes dyld to synchronously run the\n         * callback for every already-loaded image, AND triggers dyld's\n         * \"ok, init time\" pathway that dispatches each loaded image's\n         * mod_init_func. Specifically, libSystem's mod_init_func is\n         * what initialises the malloc subsystem. Without that path\n         * firing, the very first malloc/realloc anywhere in the\n         * program faults inside _malloc_initialize because libSystem's\n         * internal globals are still NULL.\n         *\n         * tcc itself never emits DWARF .eh_frame data, but we still\n         * need the side effect. So our stub registers a do-nothing\n         * callback \u2014 that's the minimum to trigger dyld's init dance.\n         *\n         * The `_dyld_register_func_for_add_image` symbol is exported\n         * by libSystem and resolved through our normal stub mechanism.\n         * (Without crt1.o populating __DATA,__dyld, this stub would\n         * itself fail; that's handled in ppc-macho.c.) */\n        if (!find_elf_sym(s->symtab, \"__keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections\")) {\n            static const char keymgr_stub[] =\n                \"extern int _dyld_register_func_for_add_image(\\n\"\n                \"    void (*func)(const void *mh, long vmaddr_slide));\\n\"\n                \"extern int _dyld_register_func_for_remove_image(\\n\"\n                \"    void (*func)(const void *mh, long vmaddr_slide));\\n\"\n                \"static void __tcc_keymgr_noop(const void *mh, long s){\\n\"\n                \"    (void)mh; (void)s;\\n\"\n                \"}\\n\"\n                \"void __keymgr_dwarf2_register_sections(\"\n                \"void *begin, void *end) {\\n\"\n                \"    (void)begin; (void)end;\\n\"\n                \"    _dyld_register_func_for_add_image(__tcc_keymgr_noop);\\n\"\n                \"    _dyld_register_func_for_remove_image(__tcc_keymgr_noop);\\n\"\n                \"}\\n\";\n            if (tcc_compile_string(s, keymgr_stub) != 0)\n                return -1;\n        }"
}

## 🤖 Claude

`_libc_initializer` at 0x9010ba64 is what we need to run. Let me check if it's callable directly via libSystem:

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho \"=== Is _libc_initializer exported? ===\"\nnm /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib 2>/dev/null | grep -E \"T _libc_init|T __libc_init|libc_init\"\necho\necho \"=== Check libSystem mod_init_func section ===\"\notool -l /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib | grep -B1 -A8 mod_init_func | head -15\necho\necho \"=== Hex dump first 32 bytes of libSystem __mod_init_func ===\"\notool -arch ppc -s __DATA __mod_init_func /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib 2>&1 | head -5'",
  "description": "Find _libc_initializer export and libSystem mod_init_func"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

Found the missing piece! crt1's `_start` calls `*0x4e4` and `*0x4ec` which point to `_mach_init_routine` and `_cthread_init_routine` (libSystem pointers). The linker normally allocates non-lazy symbol pointer slots for these and dyld populates them. Our linker doesn't, so they're zero, so crt1 skips them — and `_libc_initializer` is never called by libSystem's threading code.

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho \"=== Check addresses on this Tiger system ===\"\nnm /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib | grep -E \"_mach_init_routine|_cthread_init_routine|_libc_initializer\"\necho\necho \"=== Are these the expected slots in tcc-self __data? ===\"\notool -d /tmp/tcc-self | head -6\necho\necho \"=== Working binary __data ===\"\notool -d /tmp/tcc-self-gcc | head -6'",
  "description": "Check stability of libSystem init pointer addresses"
}


---

🔧 Using tool: Bash

## 🤖 Claude

`_libc_initializer` IS being called in both binaries! Same stack from dyld's `initializeMainExecutable`. So malloc init is being triggered. Let me see what happens INSIDE libc_initializer in our binary:

---

## 🤖 Claude

**FOUND IT!** `libc_initializer` calls `__NSGetEnviron` which returns a pointer to `_environ`. Then dereferences. **`__NSGetEnviron` returns NULL** in our binary because `_environ` isn't properly exported!

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "ssh ibookg37 '\necho \"=== _environ in BROKEN binary ===\"\nnm /tmp/tcc-self | grep \"environ\\b\\|_environ\\b\\|NXArgv\\|NXArgc\\|^00 \" | head -10\necho\necho \"=== _environ in WORKING binary ===\"\nnm /tmp/tcc-self-gcc | grep \"environ\\b\\|_environ\\b\\|NXArgv\\|NXArgc\" | head -10\necho\necho \"=== Symbol type details for _environ ===\"\necho \"broken:\"\nnm -p -m /tmp/tcc-self     | grep -E \"_environ|_NXArgc|_NXArgv\"\necho \"working:\"\nnm -p -m /tmp/tcc-self-gcc | grep -E \"_environ|_NXArgc|_NXArgv\"'",
  "description": "Check _environ visibility/export between binaries"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude

**THE ROOT CAUSE!** In the working binary, `_environ`, `_NXArgv`, `_NXArgc` are marked `[referenced dynamically]` (N_DESC flag `REFERENCED_DYNAMICALLY`). dyld uses this flag to find them via `__NSGetEnviron` etc. Our binary doesn't set the flag, so `__NSGetEnviron` returns NULL → crash.

---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Bash
Input: {
  "command": "grep -n \"REFERENCED_DYNAMICALLY\\|n_desc\\|put.*nlist\\|put_nlist\\|emit_nlist\" tcc/ppc-macho.c | head -20",
  "description": "Find nlist emission code"
}


---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Read
Input: {
  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/ppc-macho.c",
  "offset": 110,
  "limit": 15
}


---

## 🤖 Claude


🔧 Using tool: Read

  "file_path": "/Users/cell/claude/tcc-darwin8-ppc/tcc/ppc-macho.c",
  "offset": 1900,
  "limit": 62
}
